perturbation.original_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, general knowledge, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.explanation: Including 'general knowledge' within the definition of Confidential Information creates a conflict. Trade secret law protects specific, proprietary information, not general knowledge.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as adopted by most states, including Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By including 'general knowledge' in the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the agreement attempts to protect information that is explicitly excluded from trade secret protection under the UTSA, making that aspect of the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The inclusion of "general knowledge" in the definition of "Confidential Information" contradicts the established understanding of trade secrets, which are defined as information that derives economic value from not being generally known. The provided snippet from Delaware Code Online Title 6 states that a trade secret is information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. By attempting to protect "general knowledge," the modified contract text seeks to extend protection beyond what is legally recognized as a trade secret, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing contained in this Section 1 shall be deemed to prevent a Stockholder from (a) leasing or renting real property in which the Stockholder owns a direct or <PAGE> indirect interest to a person or entity engaged in the Business; or (b) beneficially owning, directly or indirectly, 5% or less of any class of securities of an entity that has a class of securities registered under Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, so long as such securities ownership does not include any operational, managerial or consulting relationship with such corporation or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing contained in this Section 1 shall be deemed to prevent a Stockholder from (a) leasing or renting real property in which the Stockholder owns a direct or <PAGE> indirect interest to a person or entity engaged in the Business; or (b) beneficially owning, directly or indirectly, 25% or less of any class of securities of an entity that has a class of securities registered under Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, so long as such securities ownership does not include any operational, managerial or consulting relationship with such corporation or entity.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the ownership percentage to 25% could be considered a material interest, potentially violating the non-compete if the entity is a direct competitor. Delaware law looks at the substance of the relationship, not just the percentage ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding fiduciary duties and breach of contract, specifically related to non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory contradiction, increasing the ownership stake could violate Delaware law's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing within the contract. A 25% ownership could grant the stockholder significant influence, potentially conflicting with the non-compete, particularly if the stockholder is aware that the corporation is acting to compete directly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc

perturbation.justification: The modification increases the allowable stock ownership from 5% to 25%. The perturbation explanation states that increasing the ownership percentage to 25% could be considered a material interest, potentially violating the non-compete if the entity is a direct competitor, which is not allowed. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating that a 25% ownership could grant the stockholder significant influence, potentially conflicting with the non-compete. While the snippets do not directly state a contradiction, they lay the ground work for directors to be in charge, and the changed text puts a stockholder in charge, which is a conflict. This contradicts the non-compete provision, as a 25% ownership stake could give the stockholder undue influence over a competing entity.

perturbation.original_text: Without intending to limit the remedies available to AUGI or the Company, each Stockholder further agrees that damages at law will be an insufficient remedy to AUGI if such Stockholder(s) breaches the terms of this Agreement and that AUGI or the Company may apply for and have injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to restrain the breach or threatened breach of or otherwise to specifically enforce any of the covenants contained herein, in each case without the necessity of proving damages or posting bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Without intending to limit the remedies available to AUGI or the Company, each Stockholder further agrees that damages at law will be an insufficient remedy to AUGI if such Stockholder(s) breaches the terms of this Agreement and that AUGI or the Company may apply for and have injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to restrain the breach or threatened breach of or otherwise to specifically enforce any of the covenants contained herein, in each case with the necessity of proving damages and posting bond.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring AUGI to prove damages and post a bond directly contradicts standard injunctive relief procedures in Delaware, which often allow for injunctions without proving monetary damages, especially for non-compete breaches where the harm is considered irreparable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Court of Chancery Rules, Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65 governs injunctions in the Delaware Court of Chancery. While the court has discretion regarding bond requirements, mandating both proof of damages and a bond contradicts the equitable nature of injunctive relief, especially in non-compete cases where the harm is often deemed irreparable and difficult to quantify in monetary terms. The standard is usually 'irreparable harm' rather than quantifiable damages.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a requirement for AUGI to prove damages and post a bond to obtain injunctive relief, which directly contradicts the standard practice in Delaware courts, where injunctive relief is often granted without proving monetary damages, particularly in cases involving non-compete breaches where the harm is difficult to quantify. The original text stated that injunctive relief could be obtained 'without the necessity of proving damages or posting bond,' while the changed text mandates 'with the necessity of proving damages and posting bond.' The provided law snippet is a 'Page Not Found' error, which prevents any direct comparison, but the Perturbation and Law Explanations strongly suggest a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified or altered to conform thereto or, if not possible, to be omitted herefrom. The invalidity of any portion hereof shall not affect the force and effect of the remaining valid portion hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed valid and enforceable to the fullest extent possible. The invalidity of any portion hereof shall not affect the force and effect of the remaining valid portion hereof.
perturbation.explanation: This clause originally provides for severability and modification to conform with law. Changing it to enforce conflicting provisions 'to the fullest extent possible' directly contradicts the principle that contracts cannot override statutory law. It aims to enforce illegal/unenforceable clauses, which is contrary to established legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding legality and enforceability, as codified in Delaware case law.
perturbation.law_citation: Wallace ex rel. Cencom Cable Income Partners II, Inc. L.P. v. Wood, 752 A.2d 1175, 1180 (Del.Ch. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware case law, such as Wallace v. Wood, establishes that contracts violating public policy or statutory law are unenforceable. This change attempts to enforce clauses conflicting with law, which directly opposes this established principle of contract law in Delaware. The previous clause aims to modify conflicting clauses where possible to conform with the law. This change actively opposes doing so.

Miscellaneous Subchapter XII. Statutory Public Benefit Limited Liability Companies
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates enforcement of contract provisions that conflict with applicable laws, which directly contradicts the established principle that contracts cannot override statutory law. The original text allowed for modification or omission of conflicting provisions. The altered version attempts to validate and enforce these conflicting provisions 'to the fullest extent possible.' While snippet 1 does not provide direct context here, the Law Explanation explains the change violates established Delaware case law.
